title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
author: James Silberman
orientation: An indictment of three self-described pro-life Oklahoma figures, Tony Lauinger, Jason Smalley, and Greg Treat, for blocking SB13 (the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act) despite a supermajority Republican legislature. The article argues their constitutional and strategic objections are either ignorant or dishonest, and that abolition requires legislators to defy Roe v. Wade.
audiences: Pro-life activists weighing incrementalism vs. abolition

questions.question: What does Oklahoma SB13 actually propose to do?
questions.answer: SB13, titled 'The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act,' would extend equal protection of state law to unborn children from the moment of fertilization and treat any contrary federal statutes, regulations, executive orders, or court rulings as void.
questions.quote: It is the intent of the Legislature to provide to unborn children the equal protection of the laws of this state; to establish that a living human child, from the moment of fertilization upon the fusion of a human spermatozoon with a human ovum, is entitled to the same rights, powers, privileges, justice and protections as are secured or granted by the laws of this state to any other human person; and to treat as void and of no effect any and all federal statutes, regulations, executive orders and court rulings, which would deprive an unborn child of the right to life.

questions.question: Why does the article say abortion remains legal in Oklahoma despite a supermajority Republican legislature?
questions.answer: Silberman names three men, National Right to Life VP Tony Lauinger and State Senators Jason Smalley and Greg Treat, as the specific obstacles. Lauinger lobbies against SB13, Smalley chairs the committee where it has been buried, and Treat presides over the Senate.
questions.quote: Abortion remains legal in Oklahoma because of National Right to Life Vice President Tony Lauinger, and because of pro-life State Senators Jason Smalley (R-Stroud) and Greg Treat (R-Oklahoma City). Lauinger, who also is President of Oklahomans for Life, has long lobbied against SB13 and previous versions of the bill. Smalley is chairman of the Oklahoma Senate Health and Human Services committee where SB13 has been assigned and Treat is the Pro Tempore of the Oklahoma State Senate.

questions.question: What is the human cost the author attributes to Smalley and Treat refusing to hear the bill?
questions.answer: Silberman estimates that roughly 5,000 babies will be murdered in Oklahoma in 2019 because the men refuse to grant SB13 a committee hearing.
questions.quote: These men, along with their allies in Oklahoma's legislature, have committed to not hearing SB13, sentencing to death the roughly 5,000 babies who will be murdered in the state this year.

questions.question: Why does the author argue Lauinger's stated objection to SB13 is suspect?
questions.audience: Pro-life activists weighing incrementalism vs. abolition
questions.answer: Lauinger opposes the bill because it would repeal the pro-life regulations he has helped pass, which the author calls either gross incompetence or proof that Lauinger prioritizes his own legislative legacy over actually ending abortion.
questions.quote: In doing so, Lauinger either outed himself as the densest lobbyist in the country, not understanding that to end abortion, you have to get rid of regulations that allow abortion, or he is admitting that his priority is not ending abortion but maintaining his own legislative victories. There is simply no other explanation for his opposition.

questions.question: How does the article rebut Treat's claim that passing SB13 could 'hurt the pro-life movement' through severability?
questions.answer: The author points out that line 19 of page three of SB13 explicitly makes the bill inseverable, so a court cannot strike down the abolition portions while preserving the regulatory repeals. He concludes Lauinger and Treat either haven't read the bill or are lying.
questions.quote: Line 19 of page three makes SB13 inseverable. Lauinger and Treat either haven't read the bill or aren't being truthful.

questions.question: What does Senator Joseph Silk say about his colleagues' constitutional reasoning?
questions.answer: Silk, the bill's author, says the senators opposing SB13 have no understanding of the Constitution and treat the courts as omnipotent, while also failing to do what they campaigned on.
questions.quote: The Senators who oppose SB 13 have zero knowledge of the Constitution and how our republic is supposed to work. They believe that the courts are all powerful and can decide whatever they like whether it is constitutional or not.  They also have no intention of doing what they campaigned on and actually protect innocent life.

questions.question: What does the author argue is the proper response when the Supreme Court legalizes murder?
questions.answer: Silberman insists that legislators' oath is to the Constitution, not the Supreme Court, and that the only acceptable response to Roe is to defy the court outright.
questions.quote: When the Supreme Court so egregiously and intentionally violates the Constitution as to legalize murder, the *only* appropriate response is to [tell the court to pound sand](https://freethestates.org/nullification-and-interposition/index.html).

questions.question: Does Silk believe SB13 would actually pass if it got a vote?
questions.answer: Yes. Silk says the Oklahoma Senate is overwhelmingly pro-life Republican in public statements and votes, and that the bill would pass a committee or full Senate vote, but Smalley and Treat are blocking it to avoid controversy.
questions.quote: If SB 13 came to a committee or full senate vote it would pass with a majority. The Oklahoma Senate is overwhelmingly pro-life Republicans in public and votes, however [Smalley and Treat] will do whatever they can to keep it from coming to a vote because they don't want to [do] something that is controversial and impactful.

questions.question: What political action does the author urge against Smalley and Treat?
questions.answer: Silberman calls for them to be primaried by candidates with the courage to defy the Supreme Court and protect unborn Oklahomans.
questions.quote: If Smalley and Treat subject their constituents to the tyranny of the Supreme Court and Oklahoma's babies to the abortionist's knife, they should be primaried by people who aren't cowards.

questions.question: How does the article characterize the broader posture of the fifty states toward the Constitution?
questions.answer: He claims every state in the union is presently in defiance of the Constitution by tolerating abortion, which means legislators who know this are mocking their oath of office and rejecting God.
questions.quote: Indubitably, all fifty states are presently in defiance of the United States Constitution. If Smalley and Treat have read the Constitution, they know this. By allowing abortion, they mock their oath of office and rebuff their Creator.

related.title: Uncovered Letter Exposes Plan to Keep Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Direct follow-up exposing Lauinger's lobbying campaign against SB13 with primary-source documentation.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: A focused indictment of one of the three men named in this article.

related.title: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
related.reason: Companion piece on the religious establishment opposition SB13 faced in the same legislative cycle.

related.title: Free the States Statement on Sen. Jason Smalley's Resignation
related.reason: Closes the loop on Smalley, another of the three men named here, after he leaves the Senate.

related.title: Anti-SB13 Campaigners Aren't Ignorant. They're Trying to Deceive You.
related.reason: Sharpens the article's claim that Lauinger-aligned opposition is dishonest rather than merely confused.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13"?
related.reason: Explains the abolitionist strategic case for refusing incremental alternatives to SB13.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Background on the constitutional theory the article invokes when telling the Supreme Court to 'pound sand.'
